title: Multiple-Film Techniques
narrationScript: Most parts are not one thickness, but every code gives you only one density window. That is the whole problem this lesson solves. For single film viewing, a typical code wants the area of interest somewhere between about one point eight and four point oh in density for X ray work, and about two point oh to four point oh for gamma. Shoot a casting with a heavy boss next to a thin web and one exposure cannot serve both. The thin section passes far more radiation, so it comes out nearly black. The heavy section starves the film and stays too light to read. Both ends land outside the window, and outside the window you have proven nothing. Put numbers on it. With iridium one ninety two, about half an inch of steel is one half value layer. Add half an inch of steel and the radiation reaching the film drops by half. Cutting exposure in half lowers the log of exposure by about three tenths. A typical industrial film working on the straight part of its characteristic curve has a gradient around four, which means that three tenths change in log exposure moves the density by more than a full unit. The window is only about two density units deep from top to bottom. Work that through and a single gamma exposure covers barely an inch of steel variation. More range than that, and something has to give. You have three levers. You can raise the energy, which flattens the difference between thick and thin and stretches the latitude, but it costs you contrast everywhere, and contrast is what finds flaws. You can accept multiple exposures, shooting the thin region short and the thick region long, which doubles your setups and your time. Or you can load more than one film in the same cassette and get it all in one shot. That last option is the multiple film technique, and it is often the smart answer. First understand the two film properties that make it work. Speed is how much exposure a film needs to reach a useful density, and it is governed mostly by grain size. Coarse grain films are fast but grainy. Fine grain films are slow, but they draw the sharpest, cleanest image. Manufacturers group films into system classes so you can compare them, and the ranking never changes. Finer grain means slower and sharper. Latitude is the range of thickness a film can hold inside the density window, and it runs opposite to contrast. A film with a steep characteristic curve gives a big density change for a small thickness change. Wonderful discrimination, narrow coverage. A flatter curve covers more thickness but says less about each bit of it. Now the technique itself. Load a fast film and a slow film together in one cassette, with lead screens, and make a single exposure balanced between the two sections. Behind the heavy section, only the fast film reaches a readable density, so that is where you read the thick part. Behind the thin section, the fast film is hopelessly dark, but the slow film lands inside the window. One exposure, full coverage, and each section gets interpreted on the film where its density is legal. Mark the films so you know which was loaded in front, because the front film runs slightly faster than the back one. The second version uses two films of the same speed. For ordinary sections you view either film alone. For the heaviest sections, where each film alone is too light, you view both films stacked together on the illuminator. Density adds when you superimpose films, because each film multiplies down the light passing through. Two films reading two point oh each give a composite of four point oh. Codes that allow composite viewing typically require each film in the stack to read at least about one point three on its own. And the technique carries a bonus. You get a duplicate record, and insurance against one film being damaged in processing. A few habits keep multi film work honest. Plan with the characteristic curves and the exposure chart in front of you, so every section of interest lands in the window on at least one film. Identify every film, and note in the technique record which film covers which section. And never call a section on a film where the density is out of limits, no matter how clear it looks. The window exists because contrast and proof live inside it. Master this and parts with wild thickness changes stop being a problem. They become one more exposure, planned a little smarter.
imagePrompt: Under a deep red darkroom safelight, a radiographer slides two sheets of industrial X-ray film with lead intensifying screens into a single flexible cassette, a stepped steel casting waiting on the bench beside the loading table.

sections.heading: One Part, One Window, Two Thicknesses
sections.body:
A radiograph only counts where its density lands inside the code window. For single-film viewing, codes commonly require the area of interest to read about **1.8 to 4.0** for X-ray techniques and about **2.0 to 4.0** for gamma. The trouble starts when one part contains two thicknesses: the thin section transmits far more radiation and trends black, while the heavy section starves the film and stays light.

Put numbers on it. With Ir-192 on steel, the half-value layer is about **0.5 in.** - every extra half inch of steel cuts the exposure reaching the film roughly in half, which drops log exposure by about 0.30. On the straight-line region of a typical industrial film the **gradient** (the slope of the characteristic curve) runs around 4, so that one step moves density by roughly **1.2**. The whole window is only 2.2 density units deep. Work it through: a single gamma exposure holds barely **an inch of steel** thickness variation inside the window. A stepped casting or a transition weld with more range than that cannot be proven with one film at one exposure - the thin end goes black while the thick end never develops a legal density. Multiple-film technique is the standard answer.

sections.heading: Sizing Up the Thickness Variation
sections.body:
Treat thickness variation as a set of parameters you measure before the shot, not a surprise you discover on the viewer:

- **Extremes and ratio.** Record the thinnest and thickest sections of interest. The bigger the ratio, the wider the exposure spread the film must absorb.
- **Radiation quality.** Higher energy flattens subject contrast - the thick and thin sections transmit more nearly alike - so latitude grows as kV rises. That is the classic single-film fix, and its price is reduced contrast everywhere, including at the flaw you are hunting.
- **Film response.** A low-gradient film stretches the window over more thickness; a high-gradient film burns through it quickly.
- **Density budget.** From the exposure chart and characteristic curve, estimate the density each extreme will produce. If both extremes fit the window, one film does the job. If not, you either raise energy and sacrifice contrast, shoot multiple exposures and sacrifice time, or load multiple films and keep both.

That last decision is the heart of this lesson: multiple films let every section land in the window somewhere, in a single exposure, without flattening the contrast of any one film.

sections.caption: Characteristic curves of a fast and a slow film: speed shifts the curve sideways, while the slope (gradient) sets how much log-exposure range - and therefore thickness range - fits inside the 1.8 to 4.0 density window.

sections.heading: Film Speed
sections.body:
**Speed** is how little exposure a film needs to reach a useful density, and it is governed mostly by **grain size**. Large silver halide grains intercept more radiation per grain, so coarse-grain films respond fast - but they draw a grainier, noisier image. Fine-grain films demand more exposure and reward you with the sharpest, cleanest rendering. The trade never reverses: **finer grain means slower means sharper**.

Manufacturers publish film **system classes** so techniques can be written around a known performance tier - from ultra-fine-grain special-sensitivity films through general-purpose classes to fast, coarser-grain films for thick sections and low-output sources. Adjacent classes typically differ by a factor of about two to four in required exposure, and the slower class always carries the finer grain and higher image quality. Two cautions when you swap films: relative speeds are quoted at a stated energy and with lead screens, and they shift as radiation quality changes, so verify against the manufacturer's data rather than assuming the ratio holds everywhere. And the job specification usually sets the minimum film class for the quality level required - you may go finer than specified, never coarser.

sections.caption: Typical film system classes - exact speeds vary by manufacturer and energy; the published data sheet governs.
rows: Maximum-sensitivity work on critical or thin sections
General code radiography - the everyday choice
Thick sections, low-output sources, screening shots

sections.heading: Film Latitude
sections.body:
**Latitude** is the range of specimen thickness a film can present inside the acceptable density band in one exposure - and it runs **opposite to contrast**. The link is the characteristic curve. A film operating where its curve is steep converts a small thickness change into a large density change: superb discrimination, narrow coverage. A flatter response covers a wider thickness span but renders each thickness difference faintly.

Remember the anatomy of the curve from your Level I work: the **base-plus-fog** floor (around 0.3), the **toe**, the **straight-line region** where the gradient is high and radiography lives, and the **shoulder** where response flattens. Where you place your densities matters because gradient rises with density for industrial films - a technique aimed at density 2.5 to 3.5 buys more contrast than one limping along at 1.8.

You can also buy latitude with **energy**: raising kV compresses subject contrast, stretching more thickness into the window. But that purchase debits every flaw's visibility. The appeal of the multiple-film approach is that each individual film keeps working on the steep part of its curve - full contrast - while the films collectively cover the full thickness range.

sections.caption: A fast and a slow film loaded in one cassette image a stepped part in a single exposure - each section is read on the film where its density falls inside the window, and a same-speed pair can instead be viewed as a composite.

sections.heading: Multiple-Film Techniques in Practice
sections.body:
Two standard arrangements cover nearly every thickness-variation job:

- **Different speeds, one exposure.** Load a fast film and a slow film in the same cassette with lead screens and balance the exposure between the sections. Behind the heavy section only the fast film reaches the window - read the thick material there. Behind the thin section the fast film is far too dark, but the slow film lands in range. Each region of the part is interpreted on the film where its density is legal, and the radiographic report says which film covers which region.
- **Same speed, composite viewing.** Load two identical films. View singly where density permits; where each film alone is too light behind the heaviest material, view both **superimposed**. Densities add when films stack - two films at 2.0 each read 4.0 together - because each film multiplies down the transmitted light. Codes that permit composite viewing typically require **each film to read at least about 1.3** by itself, and the pair doubles as a duplicate record and insurance against processing damage.

Loading details matter: the front film runs slightly faster than the back film because the back film sits behind extra absorbing layers, so identify which film was which. And the discipline that holds it together - never interpret any area on a film whose density there is outside limits, no matter how visible the image seems.

sections.title: Decision rules that hold up
sections.body: At Ir-192 energies, one film at one exposure covers roughly **an inch of steel** variation - estimate your span from the characteristic curve before the shot. Modest overrun: load a **same-speed pair** and view the heavy end composite (each film at least about 1.3). Large span: load **fast plus slow** and read each section on its in-window film. Every section of interest must land inside the density window on at least one film - and that is the only film you may call it on.

figures.title: Two-film loading of a stepped part

checkQuestions.question: An Ir-192 technique on a steel step block puts the 1.0-in. section at density 4.0. Using a half-value layer of about 0.5 in. and a film gradient of about 4, roughly what density appears behind the 2.0-in. step?
checkQuestions.explanation: An extra 1.0 in. of steel is two half-value layers, cutting exposure to one quarter - a 0.6 drop in log exposure. With gradient 4 that is a density change of about 2.4, so 4.0 minus 2.4 gives roughly 1.6.

checkQuestions.question: A fast and a slow film are loaded in one cassette to radiograph a part with thick and thin sections in a single exposure. The THIN section is read on which film, and why?
options: The fast film, because thin sections need extra speed
The slow film, because the thin section transmits the most radiation and only the slow film stays inside the density window there
Either film, because density is identical on both
Neither - thin sections always require a separate exposure
checkQuestions.explanation: The thin section passes the most radiation, driving the fast film far above the density window; the slow film, needing more exposure, lands in range there while the fast film covers the thick section.

checkQuestions.question: Two same-speed films from a double-loaded cassette each read 2.0 behind a heavy section. Viewed superimposed, the composite density is about:
options: 4.0 - film densities add when films are stacked
2.0 - density does not change with stacking
2.8 - composite density is the average plus one step
checkQuestions.explanation: Density is the log of light attenuation, and stacked films multiply their attenuations, so densities add: 2.0 plus 2.0 reads 4.0 composite.

checkQuestions.question: Which single change widens the thickness range covered in one exposure at the direct cost of radiographic contrast?
options: Switching to a finer-grain film class
checkQuestions.explanation: Higher energy flattens subject contrast, so more thickness fits inside the density window - latitude rises - but every thickness difference, including the flaw's, now produces a smaller density difference.

checkQuestions.question: Under typical code rules for composite viewing of a double-film exposure, each individual film must have a minimum density of about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Codes that permit composite viewing commonly require each film of the stack to read at least about 1.3 on its own, with the superimposed pair falling inside the acceptable viewing range.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course 2.0, IE 2.1-2.1.3
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 - film density limits and composite viewing provisions (general reference)
